Beginner.meaning: To successfully reach a goal by working for it.
Beginner.example: Our engineering team worked hard to achieve a faster app load time.

Beginner.meaning: To change your behavior or plans to fit new conditions.
Beginner.example: In tech companies, employees must adapt quickly to new tools and processes.

Beginner.meaning: To respect and appreciate someone or something.
Beginner.example: Many juniors admire leaders who stay calm during busy deadlines.

Beginner.meaning: To be able to pay for something or have the ability to do something.
Beginner.example: The company can't afford another delay in the product launch.

Beginner.meaning: To study something carefully in order to understand it better.
Beginner.example: Data scientists analyze large datasets to find useful business insights.

Beginner.meaning: To formally tell people something important.
Beginner.example: The CTO will announce the new AI platform next week.

Beginner.meaning: To feel grateful for something.
Beginner.example: Employees appreciate managers who listen to their ideas.

Beginner.meaning: To officially agree with something or accept it.
Beginner.example: The product design must be approved before development starts.

Beginner.meaning: To discuss different opinions, often with strong emotions.
Beginner.example: Developers sometimes argue about the best programming framework to use.

Beginner.meaning: To plan or organize something in advance.
Beginner.example: HR will arrange onboarding sessions for new employees.

Beginner.meaning: To believe something is true without proof.
Beginner.example: Don’t assume the user understands the interface; always design clearly.

Beginner.meaning: To draw attention or interest.
Beginner.example: Competitive salaries help attract skilled engineering talent.

Beginner.meaning: To stay away from something or prevent it from happening.
Beginner.example: We should avoid shipping untested code to production.

Beginner.meaning: To manage different tasks or responsibilities fairly.
Beginner.example: Remote employees must balance work tasks with personal routines.

Beginner.meaning: To act in a particular or acceptable way.
Beginner.example: Employees are expected to behave professionally during meetings.

Beginner.meaning: To take something temporarily and return it later.
Beginner.example: Can I borrow your laptop charger for the presentation?

Beginner.meaning: To decide something will not happen anymore.
Beginner.example: The stand-up meeting was canceled due to a server outage.

Beginner.meaning: To take hold of something, or record information clearly.
Beginner.example: UX researchers capture user feedback to improve the product.

Beginner.meaning: To mark a happy event with joy or appreciation.
Beginner.example: The team celebrated after successfully closing the funding round.

Beginner.meaning: To test someone's ability or push them to do better.
Beginner.example: Startups challenge employees to think creatively and move fast.

Beginner.meaning: To select one option from many.
Beginner.example: Developers choose frameworks based on project needs and performance.

Beginner.meaning: To make something easier to understand.
Beginner.example: Always clarify requirements before starting development.

Beginner.meaning: To try to win or be better than others.
Beginner.example: Tech companies compete to build the fastest AI models.

Beginner.meaning: To focus your attention on something.
Beginner.example: Engineers need quiet time to concentrate on solving complex bugs.

Beginner.meaning: To check and make sure something is true.
Beginner.example: Please confirm the deployment schedule before notifying the clients.

Beginner.meaning: To join two things or help people communicate.
Beginner.example: Networking events help people connect with industry professionals.

Beginner.meaning: To make someone believe or agree with you.
Beginner.example: Product managers convince stakeholders to support new features.

Beginner.meaning: To make or build something new.
Beginner.example: Designers create UI mockups before developers write code.

Beginner.meaning: To point out problems or mistakes in something.
Beginner.example: During code review, teammates may criticize poor logic to help improve it.

Beginner.meaning: To discuss different opinions about a topic.
Beginner.example: Teams often debate whether to build a feature in-house or use an existing tool.

Beginner.meaning: To make something look more attractive.
Beginner.example: Employees decorated the office for the annual tech celebration.

Beginner.meaning: To explain the meaning or details of something.
Beginner.example: The product team must define clear user requirements before development begins.

Beginner.meaning: To make something happen later than planned.
Beginner.example: Server issues may delay software deployment.

Beginner.meaning: To provide something promised or expected.
Beginner.example: Our team aims to deliver features on schedule every sprint.

Beginner.meaning: To ask for something firmly or require it.
Beginner.example: High-performance apps demand strong server capacity.

Beginner.meaning: To say something is not true or refuse a request.
Beginner.example: Security systems deny access to unauthorized users.

Beginner.meaning: To rely on something or someone.
Beginner.example: Project success depends on clear communication and planning.

Beginner.meaning: To earn something because of your actions.
Beginner.example: Hard-working employees deserve recognition and growth opportunities.

Beginner.meaning: To build or improve something over time.
Beginner.example: Software engineers develop applications that solve business problems.

Beginner.meaning: To find or learn something new.
Beginner.example: QA testers discover bugs before the product goes live.

Beginner.meaning: To show something clearly for others to see.
Beginner.example: The dashboard displays real-time metrics for the product team.

Beginner.meaning: To separate something into parts.
Beginner.example: We divide tasks among team members based on skills.

Beginner.meaning: To support or give confidence to someone.
Beginner.example: Good leaders encourage new ideas from their team.

Beginner.meaning: To participate or become involved in something.
Beginner.example: Employees should engage in team discussions to share insights.

Beginner.meaning: To amuse or interest someone.
Beginner.example: Company events are organized to entertain and motivate employees.

Beginner.meaning: To create or start something for long-term use.
Beginner.example: We need to establish a clear workflow for QA testing.

Beginner.meaning: To look closely at something to understand it.
Beginner.example: Developers examine error logs to find the root cause of issues.

Beginner.meaning: To give something and receive something in return.
Beginner.example: Team members exchange ideas during brainstorming sessions.

Beginner.meaning: To increase or grow in size or number.
Beginner.example: The company plans to expand its data engineering team.

Beginner.meaning: To believe something will happen.
Beginner.example: Clients expect prompt support and reliable software.

Beginner.meaning: To make something easy to understand.
Beginner.example: Engineers must explain technical details to non-technical teams clearly.

Beginner.meaning: To look into something new or unknown.
Beginner.example: Product teams explore user behavior before designing features.

Beginner.meaning: To share your thoughts or feelings.
Beginner.example: Employees are encouraged to express ideas during planning meetings.

Beginner.meaning: To deal with a difficult situation.
Beginner.example: We often face tight deadlines in startup environments.

Beginner.meaning: To not succeed at something.
Beginner.example: A few ideas may fail before we find the right product direction.

Beginner.meaning: To stop being upset with someone for a mistake.
Beginner.example: In healthy teams, people forgive small errors and focus on solutions.

Beginner.meaning: To manage or take care of something.
Beginner.example: Customer support teams handle user complaints professionally.

Beginner.meaning: To picture something in your mind.
Beginner.example: Great designers imagine how users will feel when using a product.

Beginner.meaning: To contain or involve something as part of a whole.
Beginner.example: The training program will include hands-on coding workshops.

Beginner.meaning: To make something bigger or more.
Beginner.example: We need to increase system capacity to handle more users.

Beginner.meaning: To affect someone's decisions or actions.
Beginner.example: Good leadership can influence a team's motivation and productivity.

Beginner.meaning: To motivate someone to do something positive or creative.
Beginner.example: Successful startups inspire young engineers to build new solutions.

Beginner.meaning: To present something or someone for the first time.
Beginner.example: The manager will introduce the new feature in tomorrow's demo.

Beginner.meaning: To put time, effort, or money into something expecting results.
Beginner.example: Many companies invest in employee training programs.

Beginner.meaning: To ask someone to join or participate.
Beginner.example: We invited industry experts to speak at our tech event.

Beginner.meaning: To include someone or something in an activity or situation.
Beginner.example: Product development involves designers, engineers, and testers.

Beginner.meaning: To form an opinion about something or someone.
Beginner.example: We should not judge ideas too early during brainstorming.

Beginner.meaning: To keep something in good condition or continue doing something.
Beginner.example: Developers must maintain clean and readable code.

Beginner.meaning: To control or organize tasks, people, or resources.
Beginner.example: Project managers manage timelines and communication between teams.

Beginner.meaning: To find the size, amount, or effect of something.
Beginner.example: We measure system performance before rolling out updates.

Beginner.meaning: To say or refer to something briefly.
Beginner.example: Please mention any blockers during the daily stand-up.

Beginner.meaning: To see, hear, or become aware of something.
Beginner.example: Users noticed a delay in page loading after the update.

Beginner.meaning: To present or provide something.
Beginner.example: The company offers flexible working hours for employees.

Beginner.meaning: To request or arrange something.
Beginner.example: We need to order new laptops for the incoming interns.

Beginner.meaning: To take part in something.
Beginner.example: Employees are encouraged to participate in team-building activities.

Beginner.meaning: To do a task or action well.
Beginner.example: Developers must perform tests before pushing code to production.

Beginner.meaning: To convince someone to do or believe something.
Beginner.example: Product managers persuade leadership to prioritize key features.

Beginner.meaning: To stop something from happening.
Beginner.example: Security teams work to prevent data breaches.

Beginner.meaning: To create or make something.
Beginner.example: Our AI models produce accurate predictions for clients.

Beginner.meaning: To commit to doing something in the future.
Beginner.example: The vendor promised to resolve the issue by tomorrow.

Beginner.meaning: To keep someone or something safe from harm.
Beginner.example: Cybersecurity tools protect company data from hackers.

Beginner.meaning: To give or supply something.
Beginner.example: The platform provides real-time analytics for users.

Beginner.meaning: To suggest something as the best option.
Beginner.example: Engineers recommend upgrading the server to improve performance.

Beginner.meaning: To make something smaller or less.
Beginner.example: We aim to reduce load times by optimizing images.

Beginner.meaning: To say no to something.
Beginner.example: The system will refuse login attempts after multiple failures.

Beginner.meaning: To need something.
Beginner.example: This project will require collaboration between multiple teams.

Beginner.meaning: To treat someone with care and fairness.
Beginner.example: It's important to respect coworkers' opinions during meetings.

Beginner.meaning: Having a duty to look after something or make decisions.
Beginner.example: He is responsible for managing the cloud infrastructure.

Beginner.meaning: To give part of something to others.
Beginner.example: Team members share knowledge to help each other grow.

Beginner.meaning: To find an answer to a problem.
Beginner.example: Developers solve bugs before releasing updates.

Beginner.meaning: To mention an idea for someone to think about.
Beginner.example: Designers suggest changes to improve user experience.

Beginner.meaning: To continue to live or exist despite challenges.
Beginner.example: Startups survive by adapting quickly to market changes.

Beginner.meaning: To help or assist someone.
Beginner.example: Our IT team supports employees with technical issues.

Beginner.meaning: To make someone feel something unexpected.
Beginner.example: The new UI update surprised users with its simplicity.

Beginner.meaning: To believe that someone or something is reliable.
Beginner.example: Clients trust us to protect their data and privacy.

Beginner.meaning: To think something is important or useful.
Beginner.example: Companies value employees who learn continuously.

Beginner.meaning: To tell someone about a possible danger or problem.
Beginner.example: System alerts warn users before servers reach capacity.

Beginner.meaning: To use something in a careless way, causing loss.
Beginner.example: Avoid waste of computing resources by optimizing queries.

Beginner.meaning: To think about something and feel curious.
Beginner.example: Many beginners wonder how machine learning models work.

Beginner.meaning: To feel anxious or think something bad might happen.
Beginner.example: Don't worry about mistakes; learning in tech takes time.

Intermediate.meaning: To officially end or stop something.
Intermediate.example: The company decided to abolish outdated policies to support flexible work.

Intermediate.meaning: To provide what is needed, or adjust to fit new needs.
Intermediate.example: Our platform must accommodate thousands of new users as we scale.

Intermediate.meaning: To collect or gather something over time.
Intermediate.example: Engineers accumulate feedback from users to plan improvements.

Intermediate.meaning: To publicly support an idea or cause.
Intermediate.example: Leaders should advocate for employee well-being and diversity.

Intermediate.meaning: To reduce or relieve a problem or pain.
Intermediate.example: Automation helps alleviate repetitive manual tasks.

Intermediate.meaning: To change or improve something, especially a document or plan.
Intermediate.example: The legal team will amend the contract before signing.

Intermediate.meaning: To expect something and prepare for it.
Intermediate.example: We anticipate higher traffic after the product launch.

Intermediate.meaning: To express ideas clearly and effectively.
Intermediate.example: A good manager must articulate the project vision to the team.

Intermediate.meaning: To evaluate or judge something.
Intermediate.example: We assess user feedback before making UI changes.

Intermediate.meaning: To say that something is the result of a specific cause.
Intermediate.example: We attribute the performance improvement to code optimization.

Intermediate.meaning: To give official permission for something.
Intermediate.example: Only admins can authorize system access for new employees.

Intermediate.meaning: To work together with others to achieve a goal.
Intermediate.example: Designers and developers collaborate to build seamless user experiences.

Intermediate.meaning: To begin something formally.
Intermediate.example: Development will commence once final approval is received.

Intermediate.meaning: To collect and organize information or resources.
Intermediate.example: The data team will compile a report on usage trends.

Intermediate.meaning: To fully understand something.
Intermediate.example: Engineers must comprehend the system architecture before contributing.

Intermediate.meaning: To admit something is true after initially denying it.
Intermediate.example: He conceded that the original design approach needed changes.

Intermediate.meaning: To imagine or create an idea in your mind.
Intermediate.example: The founder conceived the product vision during his college project.

Intermediate.meaning: To reach a final decision or judgment.
Intermediate.example: After testing, we concluded that the new server configuration works better.

Intermediate.meaning: To make something shorter or more compact without losing meaning.
Intermediate.example: She condensed the 20-page report into a one-page summary for executives.

Intermediate.meaning: To organize and carry out an activity.
Intermediate.example: We conduct weekly reviews to track project progress.

Intermediate.meaning: To follow rules, standards, or expectations.
Intermediate.example: All internal tools must conform to the company's security guidelines.

Intermediate.meaning: To deal with a problem or challenge directly.
Intermediate.example: Managers must confront conflicts early before they affect productivity.

Intermediate.meaning: To combine multiple things into one stronger system.
Intermediate.example: We plan to consolidate duplicated databases into a single platform.

Intermediate.meaning: To think deeply about something.
Intermediate.example: Before switching cloud providers, the team contemplated long-term cost impacts.

Intermediate.meaning: To say or do the opposite of what someone else says.
Intermediate.example: The new data contradicts our previous assumptions about user behavior.

Intermediate.meaning: To change something into a different form.
Intermediate.example: Our script converts raw logs into structured analytics data.

Intermediate.meaning: To organize people or tasks so things work smoothly.
Intermediate.example: Project leads coordinate between engineering and design teams.

Intermediate.meaning: To have a relationship or connection with something.
Intermediate.example: System crashes often correlate with traffic spikes during peak hours.

Intermediate.meaning: To develop something through care and effort.
Intermediate.example: Leaders cultivate a culture of learning and innovation.

Intermediate.meaning: To figure something out using logic.
Intermediate.example: Engineers deduce system failures by analyzing logs and metrics.

Intermediate.meaning: To think carefully before making a decision.
Intermediate.example: The team deliberated on whether to rewrite the legacy system.

Intermediate.meaning: To show or explain how something works.
Intermediate.example: Developers demonstrate new features during sprint reviews.

Intermediate.meaning: To obtain something from a source.
Intermediate.example: We derive insights by analyzing customer behavior data.

Intermediate.meaning: To officially assign someone or something to a task.
Intermediate.example: A senior engineer was designated as the technical lead for the project.

Intermediate.meaning: To reduce or decrease.
Intermediate.example: Good documentation diminishes confusion among new team members.

Intermediate.meaning: To reveal or make information public.
Intermediate.example: Companies must disclose security breaches to users quickly.

Intermediate.meaning: To treat people unfairly based on differences, or to recognize distinctions.
Intermediate.example: AI systems must be trained carefully to avoid discriminating against users.

Intermediate.meaning: To send something or someone to a place quickly.
Intermediate.example: Support teams dispatch technicians to handle urgent client issues.

Intermediate.meaning: To spread information widely.
Intermediate.example: We disseminate product updates to all employees via internal newsletters.

Intermediate.meaning: To add more detail or explain further.
Intermediate.example: During meetings, engineers elaborate on technical decisions to build clarity.

Intermediate.meaning: To become visible or known, especially after being hidden or developing gradually.
Intermediate.example: New security concerns often emerge when scaling systems to millions of users.

Intermediate.meaning: To give special importance or attention to something.
Intermediate.example: Product leaders emphasize user feedback during feature planning.

Intermediate.meaning: To give someone the confidence or authority to do something.
Intermediate.example: Good managers empower team members to make decisions independently.

Intermediate.meaning: To make something possible.
Intermediate.example: APIs enable developers to connect applications seamlessly.

Intermediate.meaning: To improve something and make it better.
Intermediate.example: We enhance the user experience by optimizing UI load time.

Intermediate.meaning: To give someone more knowledge or understanding.
Intermediate.example: Internal workshops enlighten employees about emerging AI trends.

Intermediate.meaning: To improve the value or quality of something.
Intermediate.example: Online learning platforms enrich education by offering practical tech courses.

Intermediate.meaning: To imagine a future possibility or outcome.
Intermediate.example: Founders envision how their product will shape the industry.

Intermediate.meaning: To increase in intensity or seriousness; or to move a problem to a higher authority.
Intermediate.example: If the issue persists, please escalate it to the DevOps team.

Intermediate.meaning: To set up or create something that will last.
Intermediate.example: The startup aims to establish itself as a leader in cloud automation.

Intermediate.meaning: To assess or judge the value or quality of something.
Intermediate.example: We evaluate different machine-learning models before deployment.

Intermediate.meaning: To go beyond a limit or expectation.
Intermediate.example: Our infrastructure must exceed performance benchmarks during peak hours.

Intermediate.meaning: To carry out a plan or action.
Intermediate.example: The engineering team will execute the release plan tonight.

Intermediate.meaning: To show or be a clear example of something.
Intermediate.example: Her leadership exemplifies our company’s culture of innovation.

Intermediate.meaning: To make a task or process easier or smoother.
Intermediate.example: Collaborative tools facilitate seamless remote teamwork.

Intermediate.meaning: To create or develop a systematic plan or idea.
Intermediate.example: Analysts formulate strategies based on customer behavior data.

Intermediate.meaning: To encourage the development of something.
Intermediate.example: Hackathons foster creativity and rapid problem-solving.

Intermediate.meaning: To make progress difficult or slow.
Intermediate.example: Poor documentation can hinder developer onboarding.

Intermediate.meaning: To recognize or find something.
Intermediate.example: Monitoring tools help identify system bottlenecks quickly.

Intermediate.meaning: To explain something clearly with examples or visuals.
Intermediate.example: Diagrams illustrate the architecture of our microservices.

Intermediate.meaning: To put a plan, system, or idea into action.
Intermediate.example: The engineering team will implement the new API in the next sprint.

Intermediate.meaning: To suggest something without saying it directly.
Intermediate.example: The bug report implied that the issue may be related to caching.

Intermediate.meaning: To force something to be accepted or put in place.
Intermediate.example: The platform will impose restrictions on suspicious logins.

Intermediate.meaning: To motivate someone by offering rewards.
Intermediate.example: Companies incentivize employees with bonuses for high performance.

Intermediate.meaning: To include something as part of a whole.
Intermediate.example: The new model will incorporate user feedback to improve accuracy.

Intermediate.meaning: To reach a conclusion based on evidence or reasoning.
Intermediate.example: From error logs, we can infer that the service crashed due to memory leaks.

Intermediate.meaning: To prevent or slow down a process.
Intermediate.example: Too many approvals can inhibit innovation in fast-growing teams.

Intermediate.meaning: To introduce new ideas, products, or methods.
Intermediate.example: Startups must innovate continuously to stay competitive.

Intermediate.meaning: To connect parts into a unified system.
Intermediate.example: We need to integrate the payment gateway with our platform.

Intermediate.meaning: To understand the meaning of something.
Intermediate.example: Data analysts interpret business trends to guide strategy.

Intermediate.meaning: To step in to stop or change something.
Intermediate.example: Admins intervene when users violate platform policies.

Intermediate.meaning: To prove something wrong or make it not valid.
Intermediate.example: New results may invalidate earlier research assumptions.

Intermediate.meaning: To examine something carefully to discover the truth.
Intermediate.example: Engineers will investigate the cause of the sudden outage.

Intermediate.meaning: To give reasons that show something is right or reasonable.
Intermediate.example: Product managers must justify resource requests with data.

Intermediate.meaning: To create or enforce laws or rules.
Intermediate.example: Governments legislate data privacy policies like GDPR.

Intermediate.meaning: To control something in a skillful or unfair way.
Intermediate.example: Developers manipulate large data sets to train AI models.

Intermediate.meaning: To help settle a disagreement between people or groups.
Intermediate.example: Team leads mediate conflicts to keep collaboration smooth.

Intermediate.meaning: To reduce the impact of something bad.
Intermediate.example: DevOps teams mitigate security risks with regular audits.

Intermediate.meaning: To observe or check something regularly.
Intermediate.example: We monitor system performance using real-time dashboards.

Intermediate.meaning: To discuss and reach an agreement.
Intermediate.example: Vendors and companies negotiate contracts before onboarding tools.

Intermediate.meaning: To make something as effective or efficient as possible.
Intermediate.example: Engineers optimize code to reduce load time and server costs.

Intermediate.meaning: To come from or start in a particular place or situation.
Intermediate.example: Most reported bugs originate from outdated browser versions.

Intermediate.meaning: To notice or understand something using your senses or mind.
Intermediate.example: Users often perceive a fast UI as a sign of high-quality software.

Intermediate.meaning: To continue to exist or keep doing something despite difficulties.
Intermediate.example: If performance issues persist, we will scale the infrastructure.

Intermediate.meaning: To come before something in time or order.
Intermediate.example: Quality checks must precede every production deployment.

Intermediate.meaning: To officially not allow something.
Intermediate.example: Company policies prohibit sharing confidential data externally.

Intermediate.meaning: To cause a reaction, often a strong one.
Intermediate.example: Unexpected UI changes can provoke negative user feedback.

Intermediate.meaning: To strengthen or support something.
Intermediate.example: Security updates reinforce protection against cyber threats.

Intermediate.meaning: To control or manage something by rules or systems.
Intermediate.example: Firewalls regulate network access to protect internal systems.

Intermediate.meaning: To copy or reproduce something exactly.
Intermediate.example: Developers replicate bugs in test environments before fixing them.

Intermediate.meaning: To examine something very carefully.
Intermediate.example: Auditors scrutinize financial data to ensure accuracy.

Intermediate.meaning: To imitate or recreate conditions for testing or training.
Intermediate.example: We simulate heavy traffic to test system resilience.

Intermediate.meaning: To encourage activity or growth.
Intermediate.example: Hackathons stimulate creativity and rapid prototyping.

Intermediate.meaning: To prove something is true with evidence.
Intermediate.example: Engineers substantiate performance claims with benchmark results.

Intermediate.meaning: To replace one thing with another.
Intermediate.example: We substituted the old database with a cloud-based solution.

Intermediate.meaning: To temporarily stop something.
Intermediate.example: We had to suspend access to the feature while fixing the bug.

Intermediate.meaning: To keep something going over time.
Intermediate.example: Startups must sustain growth by balancing innovation with stability.

Intermediate.meaning: To combine different ideas or information to create something new.
Intermediate.example: Data analysts synthesize multiple reports to form insights.

Intermediate.meaning: To change something significantly into something better or different.
Intermediate.example: AI tools transform how businesses automate tasks.

Intermediate.meaning: To confirm something is correct or acceptable.
Intermediate.example: QA teams validate new features before releasing them to users.

Advanced.meaning: Extremely bad or poor in quality.
Advanced.example: The initial system uptime was abysmal, forcing us to redesign our infrastructure.

Advanced.meaning: Strong ability to make good decisions and judge situations effectively.
Advanced.example: Her business acumen helped the startup secure major enterprise clients.

Advanced.meaning: Involving conflict or opposition.
Advanced.example: Cybersecurity teams use adversarial testing to find system weaknesses.

Advanced.meaning: Selfless concern for others’ well-being.
Advanced.example: Open-source contributions often come from a place of altruism and community spirit.

Advanced.meaning: Having mixed or uncertain feelings about something.
Advanced.example: The board showed ambivalence about entering a risky emerging market.

Advanced.meaning: Something that deviates from what is normal or expected.
Advanced.example: We detected a traffic anomaly that indicated a bot attack.

Advanced.meaning: Lack of interest, enthusiasm, or concern.
Advanced.example: Employee apathy often signals deeper cultural or leadership issues.

Advanced.meaning: Old-fashioned or outdated.
Advanced.example: The legacy billing system is archaic and urgently needs modernization.

Advanced.meaning: Strict financial discipline or reduced spending.
Advanced.example: During economic downturns, tech firms often adopt austerity measures.

Advanced.meaning: Kind, generous, and helpful.
Advanced.example: A benevolent leadership style builds trust and loyalty across teams.

Advanced.meaning: Bold and without shame, often in a shocking way.
Advanced.example: The brazen security breach exposed thousands of user accounts.

Advanced.meaning: Honest and direct, even when the truth is difficult.
Advanced.example: The founder gave a candid update on the company's challenges during the town hall.

Advanced.meaning: Providing emotional relief through open expression.
Advanced.example: Team retrospectives can be cathartic when handled respectfully.

Advanced.meaning: Secret or hidden, especially due to being unauthorized.
Advanced.example: Clandestine product leaks can damage company trust and valuation.

Advanced.meaning: Logical, consistent, and easy to understand.
Advanced.example: We need a coherent strategy across departments to execute the roadmap.

Advanced.meaning: Satisfied with the status quo, often without awareness of risks.
Advanced.example: Complacent teams fall behind in fast-moving technology markets.

Advanced.meaning: Involved with others in wrongdoing, even indirectly.
Advanced.example: Leadership becomes complicit if they ignore unethical practices.

Advanced.meaning: A guess or conclusion based on incomplete information.
Advanced.example: Early revenue forecasts were mostly conjecture without market data.

Advanced.meaning: A confusing or difficult problem.
Advanced.example: Balancing security with user convenience is a constant conundrum in tech.

Advanced.meaning: Large in quantity; abundant.
Advanced.example: The research team collected copious user feedback before redesigning the UI.

Advanced.meaning: Able to be believed or trusted.
Advanced.example: Investors expect credible financial forecasts backed by real data.

Advanced.meaning: Hidden or mysterious in meaning.
Advanced.example: The error logs were cryptic, making the debugging process difficult.

Advanced.meaning: Deserving blame or responsibility for something wrong.
Advanced.example: The vendor was found culpable for the data leak due to poor encryption.

Advanced.meaning: Difficult to handle due to heaviness or complexity.
Advanced.example: The old approval workflow was cumbersome and slowed product releases.

Advanced.meaning: A complete failure or disaster.
Advanced.example: The rushed product launch turned into a public relations debacle.

Advanced.meaning: To show that an idea or belief is false.
Advanced.example: Analysts debunked the rumor about the company shutting down.

Advanced.meaning: Proper and polite behavior.
Advanced.example: Even during heated debates, maintaining professional decorum is crucial.

Advanced.meaning: A false belief held despite evidence.
Advanced.example: Believing growth will come without marketing is a dangerous delusion.

Advanced.meaning: Quiet, modest, and reserved.
Advanced.example: Despite her demure nature, she impressed the board with her strategy.

Advanced.meaning: To take something away or prevent access to it.
Advanced.example: Cutting training budgets deprives employees of skill growth opportunities.

Advanced.meaning: Not original; copied from something else.
Advanced.example: The UI design felt derivative, lacking innovation compared to competitors.

Advanced.meaning: Careful and persistent in work or effort.
Advanced.example: Diligent testers catch edge-case bugs others miss.

Advanced.meaning: Not sincere or honest.
Advanced.example: The marketing message felt disingenuous and customers noticed.

Advanced.meaning: A large difference between things.
Advanced.example: There is often a disparity between engineering estimates and business expectations.

Advanced.meaning: To make private information known.
Advanced.example: Employees must not divulge confidential company information.

Advanced.meaning: Doubtful or questionable.
Advanced.example: The vendor’s promises sounded dubious without performance metrics.

Advanced.meaning: Extremely bad and noticeable.
Advanced.example: The platform suffered an egregious security flaw affecting thousands.

Advanced.meaning: Difficult to find, achieve, or understand.
Advanced.example: The memory leak proved elusive even after days of debugging.

Advanced.meaning: Based on observation or experience, not theory.
Advanced.example: Product decisions should rely on empirical data from real users.

Advanced.meaning: Mysterious or difficult to understand.
Advanced.example: The founder's enigmatic announcement puzzled the entire team.

Advanced.meaning: A perfect example of something.
Advanced.example: Her calm leadership during crisis was the epitome of professionalism.

Advanced.meaning: Incorrect or based on mistaken information.
Advanced.example: The report contained erroneous data due to a syncing issue.

Advanced.meaning: Understood by only a small number of people with special knowledge.
Advanced.example: The encryption algorithm explanations were too esoteric for new engineers.

Advanced.meaning: Extremely happy or excited.
Advanced.example: The team felt euphoric after closing their first million-dollar client deal.

Advanced.meaning: Extremely painful or difficult to experience.
Advanced.example: Debugging intermittent production crashes can be an excruciating task.

Advanced.meaning: Unreasonably high in price or cost.
Advanced.example: The vendor quoted an exorbitant price for cloud migration support.

Advanced.meaning: To display a quality strongly and openly.
Advanced.example: She exudes confidence during investor presentations.

Advanced.meaning: A false belief or mistaken idea.
Advanced.example: It's a fallacy to think rapid releases always compromise quality.

Advanced.meaning: Possible and practical to do.
Advanced.example: The team assessed whether the real-time processing feature was feasible at scale.

Advanced.meaning: Having strong passion or enthusiasm.
Advanced.example: She is a fervent supporter of open-source development.

Advanced.meaning: Lasting for only a short time.
Advanced.example: The spike in user traffic was fleeting and returned to normal by evening.

Advanced.meaning: Impressively large, powerful, or challenging.
Advanced.example: Building a global-scale platform is a formidable engineering challenge.

Advanced.meaning: Not serious or having little importance.
Advanced.example: We avoid frivolous features and focus on what truly benefits customers.

Advanced.meaning: Not capable of producing useful results; pointless.
Advanced.example: It was futile to keep optimizing code without fixing the architecture.

Advanced.meaning: Shockingly bad or evil.
Advanced.example: Data theft is considered a heinous violation of trust and privacy.

Advanced.meaning: Based on possible ideas or situations rather than actual events.
Advanced.example: We ran hypothetical security breach drills to test our response plan.

Advanced.meaning: Treating all sides fairly, without bias.
Advanced.example: Hiring decisions must be impartial and free from personal preference.

Advanced.meaning: Flawless and perfect.
Advanced.example: Her presentation was impeccable, leaving no questions unanswered.

Advanced.meaning: Difficult to believe or unlikely to be true.
Advanced.example: The vendor’s claim of zero-downtime migration sounded implausible.

Advanced.meaning: Not done on purpose; accidental.
Advanced.example: An inadvertent database update caused system-wide outages.

Advanced.meaning: To encourage or stir up negative behavior or strong emotions.
Advanced.example: Leaders must communicate responsibly so they don’t incite panic during outages.

Advanced.meaning: Not easily noticed or seen.
Advanced.example: A good security system often remains inconspicuous until it’s needed.

Advanced.meaning: Feeling anger because something seems unfair.
Advanced.example: Employees felt indignant when the promotion policy lacked transparency.

Advanced.meaning: Certain to happen; unavoidable.
Advanced.example: With rapid growth, scaling challenges are inevitable.

Advanced.meaning: Extremely annoying or frustrating.
Advanced.example: The infernal pop-up notifications disrupted our testing environment.

Advanced.meaning: Existing as a natural or permanent part of something.
Advanced.example: Risk is inherent in every innovative tech project.

Advanced.meaning: A feeling that restrains someone from acting freely.
Advanced.example: Workshops help new hires overcome inhibition when sharing ideas.

Advanced.meaning: Existing naturally; built-in rather than learned.
Advanced.example: Some engineers have an innate curiosity for automation and system design.

Advanced.meaning: Never satisfied; always wanting more.
Advanced.example: Modern users have an insatiable demand for faster, smarter apps.

Advanced.meaning: Curious and eager to learn.
Advanced.example: Inquisitive analysts dig deeper into patterns others overlook.

Advanced.meaning: Unable to be touched or physically measured.
Advanced.example: Brand trust is an intangible asset that drives long-term revenue.

Advanced.meaning: Essential and necessary.
Advanced.example: Cybersecurity is an integral part of product development.

Advanced.meaning: Happening at irregular intervals; not continuous.
Advanced.example: Users reported intermittent connectivity issues during peak hours.

Advanced.meaning: Examining your own thoughts and feelings.
Advanced.example: Introspective leaders learn from mistakes and improve team culture.

Advanced.meaning: Easy to understand or use without needing instructions.
Advanced.example: A successful UI feels intuitive even to first-time users.

Advanced.meaning: Impossible to reverse or undo.
Advanced.example: Once blockchain transactions are verified, they are irrevocable.

Advanced.meaning: Feeling tired or lacking energy.
Advanced.example: A poorly motivated team can become lethargic and unproductive.

Advanced.meaning: Producing a lot of profit or value.
Advanced.example: Cloud security has become a lucrative field due to rising threats.

Advanced.meaning: Very generous and forgiving, especially toward rivals or critics.
Advanced.example: He was magnanimous in victory, crediting his competitors’ hard work.

Advanced.meaning: Showing great attention to detail.
Advanced.example: A meticulous QA engineer can prevent critical production failures.

Advanced.meaning: Famous for a bad reason.
Advanced.example: The company was once notorious for poor customer support, but it has improved dramatically.

Advanced.meaning: Not aware of what is happening around you.
Advanced.example: He was oblivious to the internal security risks until the audit report came out.

Advanced.meaning: Not well-known; difficult to understand.
Advanced.example: The documentation was obscure, making it hard for new developers to onboard.

Advanced.meaning: Suggesting that something bad is going to happen.
Advanced.example: The sudden drop in system performance felt ominous before the outage.

Advanced.meaning: Extremely luxurious or wealthy.
Advanced.example: The CEO's office looked opulent compared to the open workspace.

Advanced.meaning: A situation that seems contradictory but may be true.
Advanced.example: It's a paradox that automation can both create and eliminate jobs.

Advanced.meaning: More important than anything else.
Advanced.example: Data security is paramount when building financial applications.

Advanced.meaning: Confusing and difficult to understand.
Advanced.example: The authentication failure was a perplexing issue for engineers.

Advanced.meaning: Dealing with things realistically instead of theoretically.
Advanced.example: We take a pragmatic approach by launching MVPs before full builds.

Advanced.meaning: Trying to appear more important or smart than you really are.
Advanced.example: The overly complex slide deck came across as pretentious rather than insightful.

Advanced.meaning: Producing a lot of work, results, or ideas.
Advanced.example: He is a prolific coder, shipping multiple tools every quarter.

Advanced.meaning: A response that proves an argument wrong.
Advanced.example: The CTO prepared a strong rebuttal to counter competitor claims.

Advanced.meaning: Unnecessary because it is more than needed.
Advanced.example: Redundant code slows performance and increases maintenance work.

Advanced.meaning: Able to recover quickly from difficulties.
Advanced.example: A resilient system can handle failures without impacting users.

Advanced.meaning: Careful, detailed, and thorough.
Advanced.example: Banking software requires rigorous testing before release.

Advanced.meaning: Very careful about doing the right thing; extremely honest.
Advanced.example: Scrupulous data handling is mandatory in healthcare tech.

Advanced.meaning: Clear and definite; able to be touched or measured.
Advanced.example: We focus on tangible product benefits like speed and reliability.

Advanced.meaning: Temporary; lasting only a short time.
Advanced.example: The traffic spike was transient and returned to normal levels soon.

Advanced.meaning: Showing strong passion or enthusiasm for a cause.
Advanced.example: Zealous advocates for open-source drive innovation across the industry.

Advanced.meaning: To continue something over time without weakening.
Advanced.example: We must sustain product performance even as usage scales rapidly.
